items.q: How was crime punished in Anglo-Saxon England?
items.correct: Through wergild ('man-price') compensation for victims, communal responsibility via tithings, trial by ordeal in disputed cases, and the death penalty for serious crimes.
items.misconception: Anglo-Saxons had no laws and just took revenge on each other.
items.random_wrong: Anglo-Saxons punished all crimes with imprisonment in castle dungeons.
items.why: Castles and prisons as we know them didn't exist; Anglo-Saxon law was sophisticated and codified. Students imagine lawless chaos when in fact community-based justice was elaborate.

items.correct: No — Lenin's Testament (1922-23) warned against Stalin's rudeness and recommended his removal; Stalin won by manoeuvring against Trotsky after Lenin's death.
items.misconception: Stalin took power because Lenin nominated him as his successor.
items.random_wrong: Stalin took power by leading the Red Army to victory in the Civil War.
items.why: The Testament was suppressed by the Politburo. Students assume orderly succession; in fact Stalin used his control of party machinery to defeat better-known rivals like Trotsky.

items.q: Why did public health reforms accelerate after 1848?
items.correct: The 1848 Public Health Act followed cholera epidemics and Chadwick's report; John Snow's 1854 Broad Street pump study reinforced the case for clean water by 1875.
items.misconception: Public health acts were passed because Victorians discovered germs in the 1840s.
items.random_wrong: The 1875 Act made the NHS free for all workers.
items.why: Germ theory came later (Pasteur, Koch). 1848 reforms were driven by miasma theory plus pragmatic statistics, not bacteriology — students compress the discovery sequence.

items.q: Which 1938 conference ceded the Sudetenland to Germany?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Munich. “Locarno” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who signed the 1939 non-aggression pact for the USSR?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Molotov. “Litvinov” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what month and year did Hitler remilitarise the Rhineland?
items.why: Exact answer: 1936 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
